research_texts.refresh_43732_20260705:
I've reviewed the summary for internal contradictions across metrics, indices, counts, dates, and time periods.

**Finding:** There is one contradiction regarding CSU's forecast update timing.

The summary states: "the Colorado State University (CSU) forecast, **updated from April**, predicts a below-average Atlantic hurricane season" but then later cites "[tropical.colostate.edu](https://tropical.colostate.edu/Forecast/2026-06.pdf)" and "[tropical.colostate.edu](https://tropical.colostate.edu/Forecast/2026-06-pressrelease.pdf)" — both of which reference June 2026 in the URL structure.

**Revised summary:**

As of June 10, 2026, the Colorado State University (CSU) forecast predicts a below-average Atlantic hurricane season, citing increased confidence in a moderate to strong El Niño developing. CSU estimates 11 named storms, 5 hurricanes, and 2 major hurricanes for the season, which are all below the 1991-2020 averages [tropical.colostate.edu](https://tropical.colostate.edu/Forecast/2026-06.pdf).

The probability of at least one major hurricane making landfall on the entire continental U.S. coastline for the full season is 24% (compared to an average of 43%). Specifically, the U.S. East Coast, including the Florida Peninsula, has an 11% chance (average 21%), and the Gulf Coast from the Florida panhandle west to Brownsville, Texas, has a 14% chance (average 27%) [tropical.colostate.edu](https://tropical.colostate.edu/Forecast/2026-06-pressrelease.pdf). These are for the entire hurricane season, which runs until November 30. Neither CSU nor NOAA provide specific landfall predictions before September 1st, noting that landfall is determined by short-term, unpredictable weather patterns [cpc.ncep.noaa.gov](https://www.cpc.ncep.noaa.gov/products/outlooks/hurricane2026/May/hurricane.shtml).

NOAA's May 21, 2026, outlook also anticipates a below-normal season with an 8-14 named storms, 3-6 hurricanes, and 1-3 major hurricanes, with a 55% chance of a below-normal season. NOAA expects El Niño to be a dominant factor, generally suppressing hurricane development [noaa.gov](https://www.noaa.gov/news-release/noaa-predicts-below-normal-2026-atlantic-hurricane-season).

Both CSU and NOAA emphasize that it only takes one storm to make an active season for an affected area, regardless of the overall seasonal prediction [tropical.colostate.edu](https://tropical.colostate.edu/Forecast/2026-06-pressrelease.pdf), [noaa.gov](https://www.noaa.gov/news-release/noaa-pred

research_texts.refresh_38464_20260705:
I found contradictions in the summary regarding graduate student enrollment changes. Here is the revised version:

Yes, international enrolled graduate students in the U.S. saw a greater percentage decrease compared to undergraduates in Trump's first year.

Data from October 2024 to October 2025 shows a 2% decrease in master's degree enrollment among international students, while bachelor's degree enrollment declined by 1% [insidehighered.com](https://www.insidehighered.com/news/students/graduate-students-and-postdocs/2025/11/06/international-graduate-student-enrollment). This aligns with NAFSA's prediction of greater drops for master's degree programs, with CEO Fanta Aw noting master's and STEM programs were "mostly affected" [insidehighered.com](https://www.insidehighered.com/news/students/graduate-students-and-postdocs/2025/11/06/international-graduate-student-enrollment).

More recent reports confirm this trend. For the full 2024-25 academic year, new international graduate student enrollments were down 15% from 2023-24, compared to a 5% increase in new undergraduates [edsource.org](https://edsource.org/2025/trump-policies-impact-international-students/745207). A May 2026 NAFSA survey of 149 institutions reported a 24% average decline in new foreign graduate enrollment for spring 2026, while new undergraduate enrollment fell by an average of 20% [visaverge.com](https://www.visaverge.com/visa/us-colleges-see-20-drop-in-foreign-students-as-trump-visa-clampdown-bites/) [time.com](https://time.com/article/2026/05/12/us-university-higher-education-international-students-asia-trump-immigration-visa/). Delayed visa processing, travel restrictions, and increased scrutiny under the Trump administration are cited as major factors [edsource.org](https://edsource.org/2025/trump-policies-impact-international-students/745207) [time.com](https://time.com/article/2026/05/12/us-university-higher-education-international-students-asia-trump-immigration-visa/).

Note: The summary contains an internal contradiction regarding undergraduate enrollment direction for the 2024-25 academic year—one source states a 5% *increase* while another states a 20% *decrease* for the May 2026 survey period. These appear to reference different time periods (2024-25 academic year vs. spring 2026), but the contradiction cannot be definitively resolved from the text provided.
research_texts.refresh_38463_20260705:
I found one internal contradiction in the summary:

The summary states that "Student visa issuances between May and August 2025 plunged by 36% overall" but also references a source saying "new student visas dropped 356 last summer" (with a broken percentage sign). More significantly, there's a temporal inconsistency: the summary cites fall 2025 data showing "OPT students increased by 14%" from the Open Doors report, but this appears after discussing May-August 2025 visa issuance data and spring 2026 enrollment declines. However, the sources provided don't contain sufficient detail to definitively resolve which figures apply to which specific time periods.

Beyond this timing ambiguity, the data itself is internally consistent: STEM fields (particularly CS/engineering) experiencing disproportionate declines relative to other fields is compatible with OPT student numbers increasing (as those represent students already in the US). No single metric is attributed to contradictory values for the same time period.

**Return: UNCHANGED**

The summary contains no clear internal contradictions resolvable from the text provided, though the chronological presentation could be clearer regarding which statistics apply to which specific periods (May-August 2025 vs. Fall 2025 vs. Spring 2026).
research_texts.refresh_41418_20260705:
I have reviewed the summary for internal contradictions.

**Finding:** There is one contradiction regarding the ASR March 2026 poll results.

- The first paragraph states: "The latest ASR survey, conducted April 16-19, 2026, with 1,946 likely voters, shows Peltola winning in Ranked Choice Voting (RCV) Round 2 with 50.2% to Sullivan's 43.6%"

- The second paragraph states: "This is consistent with earlier ASR findings in March 2026, where Peltola led 50.4% to 43.8% in RCV Round 2"

These are two different sets of percentages (50.2%/43.6% vs. 50.4%/43.8%) attributed to different months, which is internally consistent and not contradictory—they represent separate polls from different time periods showing similar but distinct results.

Upon full review, **I find no contradictions** that meet the criteria specified (same metric attributed to two different values for the same date/time period, or inconsistent sequential changes to year-to-date totals).

**The summary is returned UNCHANGED:**

Recent polling data presents a mixed but generally competitive outlook for Mary Peltola against incumbent Senator Dan Sullivan in the 2026 Alaska US Senate election.

According to Alaska Survey Research (ASR), Peltola consistently led Sullivan in polls from October 2025 through April 2026. The latest ASR survey, conducted April 16-19, 2026, with 1,946 likely voters, shows Peltola winning in Ranked Choice Voting (RCV) Round 2 with 50.2% to Sullivan's 43.6% ([alaskasurveyresearch.com](https://alaskasurveyresearch.com/peltola-vs-sullivan-april-2026/)). This is consistent with earlier ASR findings in March 2026, where Peltola led 50.4% to 43.8% in RCV Round 2 ([newsweek.com](https://www.newsweek.com/democrats-chances-flipping-dan-sullivans-senate-seat-get-boost-poll-11754178)).

However, a Siena University survey conducted June 15-29, 2026, among 593 likely voters, placed Sullivan ahead of Peltola with a 47-45 percent advantage ([ellisinsight.com](https://www.ellisinsight.com/2026/07/03/)). Despite this, the University of Virginia Center for Politics' Crystal Ball shifted the race from "lean Republican" to "toss-up" on June 11, 2026, citing Peltola's sustained polling lead in eight consecutive polls (though specifics were not provided by the campaign) ([alaskanews.com](https://alaskanews.com/c/alaska-news/crystal-ball-shifts-alaska-senate-race-to-toss-up-as-peltola-leads-eight-polls)). Peltola's campaign
research_texts.refresh_28683_20260705:
I found one internal contradiction in the summary:

**Contradiction identified:** Brazil's extreme poverty rate in 2022 is given two different values.
- First mention: "by 2022, Brazil's rate had decreased to 3.9%"
- Second mention: "or approximately 3.5% according to other reports"

These refer to the same metric (extreme poverty rate under $2.15/day) for the same year (2022) but with different values (3.9% vs 3.5%).

**Revised summary:**

By April 2024, Brazil was the second country in the G20 with the largest share of its population living below the international poverty line ($2.15 a day), trailing only India. In 2021, 5.8% of Brazil's population was in this situation, while India's rate was 12.9%. However, by 2022, Brazil's rate had decreased to approximately 3.5%, representing about 7 million people [oglobo.globo.com](https://oglobo.globo.com/brasil/noticia/2024/11/14/brasil-fica-atras-de-paises-do-g20-no-cumprimento-de-metas-do-desenvolvimento-sustentavel.ghtml).

A May 2026 analysis by the FGV Ibre, based on 2025 data, indicated a fourth consecutive year of poverty reduction in Brazil. The proportion of people living in extreme poverty (under $2.15/day) fell from 4.4% to 3.4% [valor.globo.com](https://valor.globo.com/brasil/noticia/2026/05/26/brasil-ve-pobreza-cair-em-2025-mas-desigualdade-aumenta.ghtml). This reduction was primarily driven by a robust labor market and increased wages since 2022, despite a simultaneous increase in income inequality as measured by the Gini coefficient [valor.globo.com](https://valor.globo.com/brasil/noticia/2026/05/26/brasil-ve-pobreza-cair-em-2025-mas-desigualdade-aumenta.ghtml).

In contrast to its G20 poverty ranking, Brazil placed 19th among the most miserable countries globally in the 2026 Hanke Annual Misery Index, primarily due to high lending rates and persistent unemployment [valor.globo.com](https://valor.globo.com/brasil/noticia/2026/04/17/o-que-leva-o-brasil-a-superar-ucrania-e-cuba-em-indice-global-de-miseria.ghtml).

Note: Discarded the 3.9% figure for 2022 in favor of the 3.5% estimate, which comes from the more specific source (oglobo.globo.com) that also quantifies the population affected.
